deck_summary_one_sentence: 11th annual Strategy& Digital Auto Report (Volume 1) presenting consumer survey insights (n=3,000 across US/EU/China) on connected/electric/autonomous mobility preferences plus strategic implications for OEMs on user interfaces, subscription models, and battery/charging ecosystems.

color_palette.notes: Classic Strategy& red palette; matches EV Sales Review template — likely shared brand asset library across Strategy& automotive practice.

chart_styling.notes: Charts use red for primary and gray/black for comparison; consistent 'Source: PwC Strategy&...' citation pattern.

layout_conventions.no_fly_zones_observed: Top-band for action titles. Bottom-band for Strategy& brand + 'Digital Auto Report 2023' + page number. Many bespoke framework/diagram slides for the 'implications for auto players' section.

slides.title_text: Digital Auto Report 2023 — What consumers really want (Volume 1)
slides.main_message: Cover slide: Digital Auto Report 2023 — What consumers really want, Volume 1

slides.title_text: Digital Auto Report 2023 — methodology overview
slides.main_message: Eleventh annual Digital Auto Report by Strategy& and PwC; consumer survey n=3,000 across US/EU/China; quantitative market outlook to 2035; executive interviews; Volume 1 covers consumer view and implications, Volume 2 covers market outlook/technology/regulation

slides.title_text: Executive summary — Volume 1
slides.main_message: Exec summary: consumer preferences (connected services WTP €20-40/mo; BEV openness varies sharply DE 35% / US 50% / CN 90%+; low L4 trust DE/US); auto implications: get UI right, rethink vehicle sales (subscription gain), go beyond vehicle (battery/charging)

slides.title_text: Contents — Volume 1
slides.main_message: Two-part contents: 1. Consumer preferences (connected, electric, automated, smart); 2. Implications for auto players (interface, subscription, charging)

slides.title_text: Overview of consumer survey — 3 regions, 3,000 respondents, 50 experts
slides.main_message: Survey overview: n=3,000 across Germany/US/China + 50 experts; balanced gender splits; age 18-34/35-54/55+ roughly equal; income brackets; expert mix 63% OEM / 16% supplier / 21% operator

slides.title_text: Safety and navigation remain as most important connected services features — on-demand car functions on the rise
slides.main_message: Connected service priorities: Safety, Navigation, Infotainment top across regions; smartphone mirroring up 27% in Germany; China shows much higher engagement across all features (76-96%)

slides.title_text: Experts rate infotainment higher than consumers do — in China, they underestimate relevance of on-demand functions
slides.main_message: Expert vs consumer view on connected services importance: experts rate infotainment higher but in China underestimate on-demand functions (-48%) and lifestyle (+130% from consumer to expert) — gap between expert and consumer perception

slides.title_text: Smartphone mirroring to the car has highest rating; Auto OEM apps for service access are less popular
slides.main_message: Connected services delivery preference: smartphone mirroring tops in all 3 regions (53-69%); auto OEM apps least popular (32-37%); strong preference for known mobile experience over OEM-specific apps

slides.title_text: Willingness to pay for connected services around €20/month in Germany and the US but twice as much in China (€40)
slides.main_message: Median WTP for full connected services bundle: Germany/US ~€20/mo, China ~€40/mo; high spread in China indicating luxury vs budget polarization; experts estimate lower than consumers

slides.title_text: Among on-demand functions, automated driving features such as traffic jam pilot / parking pilot are attracting more interest
slides.main_message: On-demand function importance: AC activation remains top (87-95%); traffic jam pilot up to 77% (significant growth from 60% in 2021); parking pilot, engine power, battery range extension all rising

slides.title_text: Experts in Germany/US attach even more importance than consumers to automated driving function attractiveness
slides.main_message: Expert vs consumer on on-demand function importance: experts bullish on engine power (+94%) and traffic jam pilot (+129% from consumer baseline); air conditioning activation surprisingly lower for experts in US/China

slides.title_text: Looking at powertrain preferences, German and US consumers stick with gasoline, while Chinese prefer BEV
slides.main_message: Powertrain preferences: US/Germany still favor gasoline (~80%) > PHEV > BEV; China inverted with BEV 93% > Hybrid > ICE; gasoline surprisingly more attractive for younger consumers

slides.title_text: Consumer acceptance of automated driving remains low in Germany and the US — more openness in China
slides.main_message: Comfort with autonomous Level 4/5: GER/US 60-70% uncomfortable, China only 15%; recovery from 2020 low but still hesitation; Level 5 (fully autonomous) more concerning than Level 4

slides.title_text: On a robo-ride, people want to be entertained or relax — in GER/US they also want to work, but in China prefer to socialize
slides.main_message: Preferred activities during autonomous rides: Relaxation and Media/Entertainment top in all regions; Work/productivity higher in GER/US; Social exchange notably higher in China; intention to use time gained went down vs 2021

slides.title_text: ~60% of US citizens want to pay less for a robo-taxi vs a driver-driven taxi; only ~5% want to pay more vs ~30% in Germany
slides.main_message: WTP for autonomous vs driver taxi: 60% in US want to pay less, 30% in Germany willing to pay more; younger Germans more willing to pay premium; 40-50% price cut from driver-driven taxis is the norm for cost-sensitive consumers

slides.title_text: Majority of respondents prefer to purchase a new or used car; but car subscription models are attracting interest
slides.main_message: Vehicle acquisition preference: purchase of new/used car still preferred (70-87%); subscription growing strongly especially in China; Germany subscription preference up to 27% from 14% in 2021

slides.title_text: Readiness for online car purchases very high in China, while rather low in Germany — the US falls in between
slides.main_message: Online car purchase willingness: China 36% comfortable with all-digital; US 22%; Germany only 10%; majority in Germany feel more comfortable with store processes

slides.title_text: Even as immediate COVID-19 risks decline, using one's own car remains popular; increasing use of shared modes in China
slides.main_message: Mobility patterns post-COVID: own car remains most convenient with highest increase in DE/US; shared modes growing in China; public transport intention increased across all regions

slides.title_text: Price and availability are by far the top drivers for encouraging consumers to use sustainable transport
slides.main_message: Drivers for sustainable transport adoption: cheaper price (45-60%), better availability (29-40%); user-friendly access strong in China; employer incentives lower priority

slides.title_text: Every country has different priorities to reduce CO2: In GER more walking, in the US switch to BEV, in CN public transport
slides.main_message: CO2 reduction behavior change: 79-98% willing to change; Germany prefers walking + cutting flights; US prefers BEV switch; China prefers public transport — different cultural priorities

slides.title_text: Contents — Section 2: Implications for auto players
slides.main_message: Section divider: transitioning to implications for auto players — interface, subscription, and charging

slides.title_text: Getting the user interface right — section header
slides.main_message: Section header: Getting the user interface right

slides.title_text: The relevant market for automotive players is expanding beyond the car itself — maintaining user access is crucial
slides.main_message: Ecosystem framework: mobility demand grouped into 10 life areas (Recreation, New Work, Personalized Pleasure, Holistic Wellbeing, Smart Environment, Rest/Relaxation, Belief, Relationships, Mobility, Demand Fulfillment, Personal Wealth); roles split into Orchestrator/Realizer/Enabler

slides.title_text: Getting the digital interface right means creating a differentiated experience for diverse customer needs
slides.main_message: Differentiated experience examples spanning Luxury (Bentley bespoke services, Rolls Royce digital opulence, NIO EP House, BMW 7 Series welcome lighting) vs Convenience (Mercedes me app, FINN, Uber, Sixt, Genesis pickup service)

slides.title_text: A value-creating digital service portfolio requires automotive players to balance multiple trade-offs
slides.main_message: Digital portfolio scope across four categories (Vehicle Function-as-a-Service, Consumer onboard services, Consumer offboard services, B2B/data services) with examples mapped to Mobility/Entertainment/Work/Health; portfolio trade-offs framework

slides.title_text: Along the value chain and vehicle life cycle, digital services unlock value beyond direct user monetization
slides.main_message: Value levers of digital services: Top-line (services monetization, brand loyalty, post-purchase activation, platform/data sales) and Bottom-line (R&D optimization, recall prevention, variant management, parts inventory); 30-40% additional revenue potential

slides.title_text: OEMs are forced to partner with technology players to deliver compelling digital services — risking a loss of control
slides.main_message: 5-stage spectrum of OEM-tech partnership: I) No tech involvement (full OEM control), II) OS supply, III) Content mirroring, IV) Tech player content using vehicle data, V) External development; trade-off between OEM control and customer UX

slides.title_text: Rethinking vehicle sales — section header
slides.main_message: Section header: Rethinking vehicle sales

slides.title_text: Subscription fills the gap between leasing and rental offerings — resulting overall in four major vehicle ownership archetypes
slides.main_message: Four vehicle ownership archetypes compared (Leasing, Subscription, Rental, Sharing) across duration, services included, relative price; subscription occupies middle ground with high convenience

slides.title_text: As alternative ownership models such as subscription emerge, OEMs need to sharpen their vehicle lifecycle mgmt skills
slides.main_message: Subscription customer + asset journey example showing 5 lifecycle phases over 9 years: New car leasing → Subscription → New subscription → Used car leasing → Car sharing/rental, with maintenance, refurbishment, and contract events

slides.title_text: Holistic vehicle lifecycle management aims to increase revenue and utilization, especially during 2nd and 3rd phase
slides.main_message: Subscription '3x3' asset lifecycle: 1st phase (yrs 1-3) regular maintenance; 2nd phase (yrs 4-6) sporadic refurbishment; 3rd phase (yrs 7-9) lower residual; ownership models suitable per phase with revenue and utilization potential

slides.title_text: Alternative ownership models are on the rise and offer profit potential for OEMs — if the asset life cycle is managed well
slides.main_message: Europe ownership model split 2023: Purchase 42%, Leasing 45%, Sharing 10%, Subscription 2%, Rental 1%; subscription projected to grow to 2-4m units by 2035; leasing to 7-8m units; profitability 5-7% purchase vs 10-15% leasing/subscription/rental

slides.title_text: More flexible ownership models offer benefits and risks for OEMs and customers — a win-win solution is required
slides.main_message: Subscription benefit/risk analysis from OEM and customer perspectives: customer flexibility/peace of mind vs admin pricing opacity; OEM existing network advantage vs residual value risk requiring strong operational excellence

slides.title_text: Going beyond the vehicle — section header
slides.main_message: Section header: Going beyond the vehicle

slides.title_text: Rise of e-mobility provides ample opportunities to capture value beyond the vehicle — e.g. with batteries and charging
slides.main_message: Value pools beyond the vehicle in e-mobility: Battery value chain (production, in-car assembly, exchange, second life, recycling), Charging value chain (equipment manufacturing, site/asset ownership, CPO, energy provisioning), Adjacent services (fleet mgmt, last mile, location commerce)

slides.title_text: Infrastructure and vehicle penetration are key requirements for successful realization of bi-directional charging use cases
slides.main_message: Germany bi-directional charging market simulation: 2.7M bi-directional sockets by 2030 (parking 1.8M, home/apartments 0.7M, hubs 0.16M, office 0.03M); 5M bi-directional vehicles by 2030 — share of total EV fleet growing 11%→63% by 2035

slides.title_text: Front-of-meter prosumer use cases depend on a multitude of external factors that limit mainstream adoption in short term
slides.main_message: Bi-directional charging business model comparison (V2L, V2H/B, V2G/VGI): customer demand, energy tech, regulation, economics, use cases each rated for behind-the-meter vs front-of-meter; software enabler revenue potential €160-220M behind / €470-550M front by 2030

slides.title_text: Realization and scale-up of prosumer use cases require efficient charging and battery stakeholder coordination
slides.main_message: Charging & battery ecosystem stakeholder activation diagram: end users, parking providers, CPOs, battery manufacturers, vehicle manufacturers, utility sector, service providers, software suppliers; main scale-up challenges include stakeholder fear of losing control, high transaction costs, conflicting priorities

slides.title_text: Implication for automotive players: Holistic ecosystem approach beyond core business is key to future success
slides.main_message: Four success factors for ecosystem approach: be clear about own role (orchestrator/realizer/enabler), build offering portfolio with resource allocation, maintain holistic iterative selection approach, actively manage portfolio with multi-layered ecosystem logic — balanced with lock-in benefits and management complexity

slides.title_text: Network contacts
slides.main_message: Network contacts: Jörg Krings (Automotive Europe), Jörn Neuhausen (Alternative Powertrains), Jonas Seyfferth (Connected Smart Mobility), Andreas Gissler (Digital Transformation), Hartmut Güthner (Automated Driving), regional leaders for Japan/US/China, plus contributors

provenance.input_json_source: PDF text extraction via web_fetch (no structural script run)
provenance.confidence_notes: HIGH confidence on slide_purpose, message_type, main_message — full PDF text extracted. MEDIUM confidence on density/word counts (estimated). Structural fields unknown (no .pptx). Slide count is ~40 based on visible page markers. This is a flagship Strategy& annual report — heavy on bespoke framework graphics (sections II-III) which means many slides marked Tier 3 for retrieval matching. The 'Implications for auto players' section (slides 22-38) is dominated by Strategy&-typical framework graphics like ecosystem maps, value chains, and trade-off matrices — these are high-value for inspiration retrieval but hard to swap content into via Mechanism B.
